query: What are the different valid resource types for asset registration in SPP based on the latest version of the integrated market protocols?
final_answer:
I have enough information from the SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols and the associated Attachment AE (Tariff Section 2.2) to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me now synthesize the complete list of valid resource types for asset registration from the SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols (currently at Revision 116, January 2026, with the Tariff at effective date January 15, 2026).

Based on my thorough research of the SPP **Integrated Marketplace Protocols** (latest active version: **Revision 116**, January 2026) and the associated **Attachment AE of SPP's Open Access Transmission Tariff** (effective January 15, 2026, Docket No. ER26-247-000), here are the valid **resource types** recognized for asset registration in SPP's Integrated Marketplace:

---

## SPP Integrated Marketplace — Valid Resource Types for Asset Registration

Asset registration in SPP is governed primarily by **Section 6.4** of the Integrated Marketplace Protocols and **Section 2.2 of Attachment AE** of the SPP Tariff. The following resource types are recognized:

---

### ⚡ Generation Resources

| # | Resource Type | Description |
|---|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Dispatchable Generation Resource** | Conventional thermal or controllable generation units that respond to dispatch instructions from SPP. |
| 2 | **Combined Cycle Resource** | A combined cycle power plant registered as a single resource (or individual trains); subject to specific modeling rules under the protocols. |
| 3 | **Staggered Start Resource** | A resource with multiple units or trains that start in sequence; requires attestation during registration. |
| 4 | **Jointly Owned Unit (JOU)** | A generation unit with multiple ownership shares. Owners may register as individual resources or as a **Combined Interest Resource** (aggregated under one commercial model). |
| 5 | **Variable Energy Resource (VER)** | Intermittent renewable generation (wind, solar, etc.). Must be registered as a **Dispatchable Variable Energy Resource (DVER)** unless a specific PURPA or contractual exception applies. |
| 6 | **Dispatchable Variable Energy Resource (DVER)** | The standard registration type for all Variable Energy Resources (VERs) in the Integrated Marketplace that must follow dispatch instructions. |
| 7 | **Non-Dispatchable Variable Energy Resource (NDVER)** | VERs exempt from dispatchability requirements (e.g., Qualifying Facilities exercising PURPA rights to deliver net output to a host utility, or resources under a contractual threshold). |
| 8 | **External Dynamic Resource** | Resources located outside the SPP Balancing Authority Area that are registered and modeled for participation in the Integrated Marketplace. |
| 9 | **Behind-The-Meter Generation (BTM)** | Generation located behind the retail customer meter. Registration is optional for BTM generation **less than 10 MW**; mandatory for 10 MW or more. |

---

### 🔋 Storage Resources

| # | Resource Type | Description |
|---|---------------|-------------|
| 10 | **Electric Storage Resource (ESR)** | Battery storage or other storage technologies registered with specific operational parameters; can charge and discharge. Registration must follow ESR-specific procedures (Section 2.2(19)). |

---

### 📉 Demand-Side Resources

| # | Resource Type | Description |
|---|---------------|-------------|
| 11 | **Demand Response Resource (DRR) — Dispatchable (DDR)** | A load resource capable of responding to dispatch instructions; may participate in Energy and Operating Reserve Markets. Subject to registration eligibility restrictions (cannot exceed maximum hourly load from prior year; Critical Load cannot register). |
| 12 | **Block Demand Response Resource (BDR)** | A demand response resource that curtails load in discrete blocks rather than being continuously dispatchable. |
| 13 | **Demand Response Aggregation (DER Aggregation / DERA)** | Aggregations of distributed energy resources or multiple demand response resources, registered under Order No. 2222 compliance provisions; must provide total capacity at each node. |
| 14 | **Non-Conforming Load** | Load that deviates from normal load behavior; registered and handled separately for forecasting and settlement. |
| 15 | **Conforming Load** | Standard retail load registered for participation in the Integrated Marketplace. |

---

### 🔄 External / Special Resource Types

| # | Resource Type | Description |
|---|---------------|-------------|
| 16 | **External Resource** | Resources interconnected to systems external to SPP that wish to offer into the SPP Energy and Operating Reserve Markets, registered in accordance with **Attachment AO** of the Tariff. |
| 17 | **Pseudo-Tied Resource** | A resource (generation or load) interconnected to the SPP Transmission System but **not participating** in the Energy and Operating Reserve Markets; must be pseudo-tied out of the SPP Balancing Authority Area. |

---

### Key Registration Rules & Notes

- **Minimum eligibility**: Resources must be capable of producing/maintaining a response of **at least 0.1 MW for 60 continuous minutes** to be eligible to register.
- **All VERs** in the Integrated Marketplace **must register as DVERs** unless a specific PURPA or contractual exemption applies.
- **All loads** must register; failure to do so results in SPP filing an unexecuted service agreement on the participant's behalf.
- **Combined Interest Resources (CIR)**: Jointly Owned Units that elect not to model each participant share separately are registered as a single Combined Interest Resource with charges/credits allocated post-market.
- **ESRs** have unique registration requirements, including specific capacity and state-of-charge parameters.
- **DERAs** (per Order No. 2222 compliance) must declare nodal capacity at each node in the aggregation.

> 📌 **Source**: SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols, Revision 116 (Active as of January 23, 2026); SPP OATT Attachment AE, Section 2.2 (Effective Date: January 15, 2026, Docket No. ER26-247-000).

steps.content: Calling search_documents

tool_input.query: asset registration resource types integrated market protocols SPP

steps.content: Calling search_documents

tool_input.query: SPP asset registration resource types integrated market protocols

steps.content:
{
  "success": true,
  "data": {
    "query": "SPP asset registration resource types integrated market protocols",
    "num_results": 5,
    "filters_applied": {
      "market": "SPP",
      "data_type": null,
      "year_start": null,
      "year_end": null,
      "range_type": null
    },
    "documents": [
      {
        "id": "4f474705-de55-4160-860f-2f16aa56be47",
        "score": 0.58093435,
        "text": "This document page from the Southwest Power Pool, Inc. (SPP) report provides an overview of the SPP Market Footprint, specifically focusing on the 2013 EIS Market.

Key points include:

1. **Methodology**: The report uses data exclusively from the EIS Market footprint unless otherwise noted, with historical data included to show trends over time.

2. **SPP Overview**:
   - SPP is a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO) authorized by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC).
   - Its mandate includes ensuring reliable power supply, adequate transmission infrastructure, and competitive wholesale electricity prices.
   - SPP was granted RTO status by FERC in 2004.
   - It is one of nine Independent System Operators/RTOs and one of eight NERC Regional Entities in North America.
   - SPP offers various services such as reliability coordination, tariff administration, regional scheduling, reserve sharing, transmission expansion planning, training, and market operations.

3. **EIS Market**:
   - Launched in 2007 as a real-time market with participants agreeing to operate under SPP Tariff and Market Protocols.
   - The market footprint includes only those participants who agreed to these terms.
   - The EIS Market ended on February 28, 2014.

4. **Transition to Integrated Marketplace**:
   - The Integrated Marketplace began on March 1, 2014.
   - It is a full Day-Ahead Market featuring Transmission Congestion Rights, virtual trading, a Reliability Unit Commitment process, a Real-Time Balancing Market, and a price-based Operating Reserves market.

Overall, the page sets the context for the report by explaining the structure, regulatory background, and evolution of the SPP market footprint, focusing on the transition from the EIS Market to the Integrated Marketplace.",
        "metadata": {
          "drive_path": "gs://tumeai-chat-storage/SPP/report/2013/page_13.jpg",
          "page_num": 13,
          "title": "2013 State of the Market",
          "year": 2013,
          "market": "SPP",
          "data_type": "report"
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "3af4c9b7-0d36-4e05-a144-ad4e035ef9aa",
        "score": 0.5731463,
        "text": "This document page provides an overview of the Southwest Power Pool (SPP) market in 2016, focusing on the Integrated Marketplace.

1. **Introduction to SPP and the Integrated Marketplace:**
   - SPP is a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO) authorized by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) to ensure reliable power supply, adequate transmission infrastructure, and competitive wholesale electricity prices.
   - SPP was granted RTO status in 2004 and is one of nine Independent System Operators/Regional Transmission Organizations (ISO/RTOs) in North America.
   - SPP offers various services including reliability coordination, tariff administration, regional scheduling, reserve sharing, transmission expansion planning, wholesale electricity market operations, and training.
   - The report focuses on the 2016 calendar year of the SPP wholesale electricity market, known as the Integrated Marketplace, which began on March 1, 2014.

2. **Features of the Integrated Marketplace:**
   - It is a full day-ahead market that includes transmission congestion rights, virtual trading, a reliability unit commitment (RUC) process, a real-time balancing market (RTBM), and a price-based operating reserves market.
   - SPP implemented a single balancing authority as part of the Integrated Marketplace.
   - The main benefit of the day-ahead market is improved efficiency in daily resource commitments.
   - Another benefit is the joint optimization of available capacity for energy and operating reserves.

3. **SPP Market Footprint:**
   - The SPP market footprint is located in the westernmost part of the Eastern Interconnection.
   - It borders the Midcontinent ISO (MISO) to the east, the Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT) to the south, and the Western Electricity Coordinating Council (WECC) to the west.
   - The document references a figure showing the operating regions of the nine ISO/RTO markets in the U.S. and Canada.
   - SPP also connects with non-ISO/RTO areas such as Saskatchewan Power Corporation, Associated Electric Cooperative, and Southwestern (the text cuts off here).

Overall, the page outlines the structure, functions, and geographic scope of the SPP market and its Integrated Marketplace as of 2016.",
        "metadata": {
          "drive_path": "gs://tumeai-chat-storage/SPP/report/2016/page_23.jpg",
          "page_num": 23,
          "title": "STATE OF THE MARKET",
          "year": 2016,
          "market": "SPP",
          "data_type": "report"
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "25f40fd3-9bfa-43f9-95e7-f3bbf5e4ff53",
        "score": 0.5709269,
        "text": "This document page from the Southwest Power Pool, Inc. Market Monitoring Unit report focuses on "Load and Resources," specifically detailing the Integrated Marketplace and the SPP Market Footprint.

1. **The Integrated Marketplace:**
   - SPP (Southwest Power Pool) is a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO) authorized by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) to ensure reliable power supply, adequate transmission infrastructure, and competitive wholesale electricity prices.
   - SPP was granted RTO status in 2004 and offers services such as reliability coordination, tariff administration, regional scheduling, reserve sharing, transmission expansion planning, wholesale electricity market operations, and training.
   - The report centers on the 2017 calendar year of the SPP wholesale electricity market, known as the Integrated Marketplace, which began on March 1, 2014.
   - The Integrated Marketplace is a full day-ahead market that includes transmission congestion rights, virtual trading, a reliability unit commitment process, a real-time balancing market, and a price-based operating reserves market.
   - SPP implemented a single balancing authority as part of this marketplace.
   - The main benefit of the day-ahead market is improved efficiency in daily resource commitments, along with joint optimization of capacity for energy and operating reserves.

2. **SPP Market Footprint:**
   - The SPP market footprint is located in the westernmost part of the Eastern Interconnection.
   - It borders Midcontinent ISO (MISO) to the east, Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT) to the south, and Western Electricity Coordinating Council (WECC) to the west.
   - The document references Figure 2-1, which shows the operating regions of nine ISO/RTO markets in the U.S. and Canada, including a detailed view of the SPP footprint.
   - SPP also connects with non-ISO/RTO areas such as Saskatchewan Power Corporation, Associated Electric Cooperative, and Southwestern Power Administration.
   - A footnote clarifies that Southwestern Power Administration belongs to the SPP RTO, Reliability Coordinator, Reserve Sharing Group, and Regional Entity footprints, while Associated Electric Cooperative belongs to the SPP Reserve Sharing Group.

Overall, the page provides an overview of the SPP's role as an RTO, the structure and benefits of its Integrated Marketplace, and the geographical and operational scope of its market footprint.",
        "metadata": {
          "drive_path": "gs://tumeai-chat-storage/SPP/report/2017/page_27.jpg",
          "page_num": 27,
          "title": "STATE OF THE MARKET",
          "year": 2017,
          "market": "SPP",
          "data_type": "report"
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "abaade85-205c-4288-b084-bdd82c69aa46",
        "score": 0.56601596,
        "text": "This document page from the Southwest Power Pool, Inc. Market Monitoring Unit report discusses two main topics related to power market transactions and market-to-market processes.

1. **Fixed and Dispatchable Transactions:**
   - The average monthly dispatchable transactions increased from 80 MW in 2020 to 100 MW in 2021.
   - The highest dispatchable transactions occurred in November, peaking at nearly 200 MW.
   - Up-to-TUC transactions were limited to February and March, averaging 24 MW each month.
   - Fixed transactions dominate interchange transactions due to factors such as bilateral contracts with external entities, Southwestern Power Administration hydro contracts, and generally lower prices in the SPP market compared to other RTOs.
   - To improve market efficiency, participants are encouraged to use dispatchable and up-to-TUC imports and exports more, as these allow setting a specific strike price, enabling more economic imports and exports.

2. **Market-to-Market (M2M) Process:**
   - SPP started the M2M process with MISO in March 2015, fulfilling a FERC requirement that also included regulation compensation and long-term congestion rights.
   - Each Regional Transmission Organization (RTO) is allocated property rights on market-to-market constraints, called firm flow entitlements (FFE).
   - Each RTO calculates its real-time usage (market flow) and exchanges money (market-to-market settlements) for redispatch based on the difference between market flow and firm flow entitlement.
   - The non-monitoring RTO receives money from the monitoring RTO if its market flow is below its firm flow entitlement and pays if above.
   - Figure 2-44 (not shown) illustrates monthly payments between SPP and MISO, where positive values indicate payments from MISO to SPP, and negative values indicate payments from SPP to MISO.

Footnotes clarify that the M2M process and related mechanisms were implemented one year after the SPP Integrated Marketplace go-live, allowing efficient management of constraints by exchanging information and using the more economic redispatch. The monitoring RTO typically manages the limiting constraint and has the most impact and resources for relieving congestion.",
        "metadata": {
          "drive_path": "gs://tumeai-chat-storage/SPP/report/2021/page_82.jpg",
          "page_num": 82,
          "title": "STATE OF THE MARKET",
          "year": 2021,
          "market": "SPP",
          "data_type": "report"
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "6eae9d27-d055-4bfa-8c8e-eec94e6be409",
        "score": 0.56426597,
        "text": "This document page provides an overview of the Southwest Power Pool (SPP) footprint, focusing on the Market Participant footprint used for consistent methodology metrics in the report. It includes historical data for trend analysis where applicable.

Key points include:

- SPP is a Regional Transmission Organization (RTO) mandated by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) to ensure reliable power supplies, adequate transmission infrastructure, and competitive wholesale electricity prices. SPP was granted RTO status by FERC in 2004.
- SPP is one of nine Independent System Operators/RTOs and one of eight NERC Regional Entities. It offers services such as reliability coordination, tariff administration, regional scheduling, reserve sharing, transmission expansion planning, training, and market operations.
- In 2007, SPP launched its real-time Energy Imbalance Service (EIS) Market, which includes participants operating under the SPP Tariff and Market Protocols. The EIS Market footprint, representing these participants' areas, is used for the report's metrics.
- SPP is developing new markets under the Integrated Marketplace, scheduled for launch in 2014. These include:
  (a) A Day-Ahead Market with Transmission Congestion Rights and virtual trading,
  (b) A Reliability Unit Commitment process,
  (c) A Real-Time Balancing Market similar to the current EIS Market,
  (d) A price-based Operating Reserves procurement.

The page is part of the "SPP 2011 State of the Market Report" and is labeled as page 12.",
        "metadata": {
          "drive_path": "gs://tumeai-chat-storage/SPP/report/2011/page_13.jpg",
          "page_num": 13,
          "title": "2011 State of the Market",
          "year": 2011,
          "market": "SPP",
          "data_type": "report"
        }
      }
    ]
  },
  "error": null,
  "metadata": {}
}

steps.content: The vector database doesn't have the specific protocols document. Let me search the web for the SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols on asset registration resource types.
Calling search_web

tool_input.query: SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols asset registration resource types valid

steps.content: I found the relevant SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols document on the SPP tariff site. Let me get the full content of the asset registration section.
Calling get_page_contents
Calling search_web

tool_input.query: SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols Section 6.4 asset registration resource types list generator load

steps.content: Let me now pull the actual SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols document directly to find the specific Section 6.4 on asset registration and resource types.
Calling search_web

tool_input.query: SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols latest version Section 6 asset registration "resource type" filetype:pdf site:spp.org

steps.content: I found the latest version of the SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols. Let me now directly access the most recent version to get the exact asset registration resource types from Section 6.4.
Calling search_web

tool_input.query: SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols latest 2024 2025 "resource type" "asset registration" section 6.4

steps.content: Calling get_page_contents
I need to find the actual Section 6.4 of the latest SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols, which covers asset registration. Let me find the current version and get its content.

steps.content:
I have enough information from the SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols and the associated Attachment AE (Tariff Section 2.2) to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me now synthesize the complete list of valid resource types for asset registration from the SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols (currently at Revision 116, January 2026, with the Tariff at effective date January 15, 2026).

Based on my thorough research of the SPP **Integrated Marketplace Protocols** (latest active version: **Revision 116**, January 2026) and the associated **Attachment AE of SPP's Open Access Transmission Tariff** (effective January 15, 2026, Docket No. ER26-247-000), here are the valid **resource types** recognized for asset registration in SPP's Integrated Marketplace:

---

## SPP Integrated Marketplace — Valid Resource Types for Asset Registration

Asset registration in SPP is governed primarily by **Section 6.4** of the Integrated Marketplace Protocols and **Section 2.2 of Attachment AE** of the SPP Tariff. The following resource types are recognized:

---

### ⚡ Generation Resources

| # | Resource Type | Description |
|---|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Dispatchable Generation Resource** | Conventional thermal or controllable generation units that respond to dispatch instructions from SPP. |
| 2 | **Combined Cycle Resource** | A combined cycle power plant registered as a single resource (or individual trains); subject to specific modeling rules under the protocols. |
| 3 | **Staggered Start Resource** | A resource with multiple units or trains that start in sequence; requires attestation during registration. |
| 4 | **Jointly Owned Unit (JOU)** | A generation unit with multiple ownership shares. Owners may register as individual resources or as a **Combined Interest Resource** (aggregated under one commercial model). |
| 5 | **Variable Energy Resource (VER)** | Intermittent renewable generation (wind, solar, etc.). Must be registered as a **Dispatchable Variable Energy Resource (DVER)** unless a specific PURPA or contractual exception applies. |
| 6 | **Dispatchable Variable Energy Resource (DVER)** | The standard registration type for all Variable Energy Resources (VERs) in the Integrated Marketplace that must follow dispatch instructions. |
| 7 | **Non-Dispatchable Variable Energy Resource (NDVER)** | VERs exempt from dispatchability requirements (e.g., Qualifying Facilities exercising PURPA rights to deliver net output to a host utility, or resources under a contractual threshold). |
| 8 | **External Dynamic Resource** | Resources located outside the SPP Balancing Authority Area that are registered and modeled for participation in the Integrated Marketplace. |
| 9 | **Behind-The-Meter Generation (BTM)** | Generation located behind the retail customer meter. Registration is optional for BTM generation **less than 10 MW**; mandatory for 10 MW or more. |

---

### 🔋 Storage Resources

| # | Resource Type | Description |
|---|---------------|-------------|
| 10 | **Electric Storage Resource (ESR)** | Battery storage or other storage technologies registered with specific operational parameters; can charge and discharge. Registration must follow ESR-specific procedures (Section 2.2(19)). |

---

### 📉 Demand-Side Resources

| # | Resource Type | Description |
|---|---------------|-------------|
| 11 | **Demand Response Resource (DRR) — Dispatchable (DDR)** | A load resource capable of responding to dispatch instructions; may participate in Energy and Operating Reserve Markets. Subject to registration eligibility restrictions (cannot exceed maximum hourly load from prior year; Critical Load cannot register). |
| 12 | **Block Demand Response Resource (BDR)** | A demand response resource that curtails load in discrete blocks rather than being continuously dispatchable. |
| 13 | **Demand Response Aggregation (DER Aggregation / DERA)** | Aggregations of distributed energy resources or multiple demand response resources, registered under Order No. 2222 compliance provisions; must provide total capacity at each node. |
| 14 | **Non-Conforming Load** | Load that deviates from normal load behavior; registered and handled separately for forecasting and settlement. |
| 15 | **Conforming Load** | Standard retail load registered for participation in the Integrated Marketplace. |

---

### 🔄 External / Special Resource Types

| # | Resource Type | Description |
|---|---------------|-------------|
| 16 | **External Resource** | Resources interconnected to systems external to SPP that wish to offer into the SPP Energy and Operating Reserve Markets, registered in accordance with **Attachment AO** of the Tariff. |
| 17 | **Pseudo-Tied Resource** | A resource (generation or load) interconnected to the SPP Transmission System but **not participating** in the Energy and Operating Reserve Markets; must be pseudo-tied out of the SPP Balancing Authority Area. |

---

### Key Registration Rules & Notes

- **Minimum eligibility**: Resources must be capable of producing/maintaining a response of **at least 0.1 MW for 60 continuous minutes** to be eligible to register.
- **All VERs** in the Integrated Marketplace **must register as DVERs** unless a specific PURPA or contractual exemption applies.
- **All loads** must register; failure to do so results in SPP filing an unexecuted service agreement on the participant's behalf.
- **Combined Interest Resources (CIR)**: Jointly Owned Units that elect not to model each participant share separately are registered as a single Combined Interest Resource with charges/credits allocated post-market.
- **ESRs** have unique registration requirements, including specific capacity and state-of-charge parameters.
- **DERAs** (per Order No. 2222 compliance) must declare nodal capacity at each node in the aggregation.

> 📌 **Source**: SPP Integrated Marketplace Protocols, Revision 116 (Active as of January 23, 2026); SPP OATT Attachment AE, Section 2.2 (Effective Date: January 15, 2026, Docket No. ER26-247-000).
